fuzzy_prd: We're building a small set of helper utilities for tools that work with cluster configuration records. The main pain point is that we keep re-implementing the same fiddly low-level operations in every tool: making bulk edits to nested configuration documents, deciding whether a resource matches a selection filter, figuring out when a workload was actually running versus just defined, and cleaning up the machine-managed bookkeeping fields before re-applying a record. Without a shared place for this logic, different tools give different answers for edge cases — especially around selector rules and the tricky question of 'is this collection entry still running?' We want these handled the same way as our existing resource-lifecycle module. Each utility should be a pure function: same input always gives the same output, nothing external. The bulk-edit helper needs a way to target every item in a list at once using a wildcard, and it must handle the case where the target key already exists — sometimes we want to overwrite it, sometimes leave it alone. The filter matcher needs to support both 'match one of these values' and 'does this key exist at all' style checks. The running-interval logic needs to collapse all the per-container start and end times into one clean interval, and only consider the job finished once every main container is done. Deliverable is a clean library plus a CLI adapter that reads commands from stdin and writes results to stdout.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: pub fn add(path: &str, key: &str, value: &Value, obj: &mut Value, overwrite: bool) -> EmptyResult

trigger_keywords: how is the document output formatted
what format should the JSON output be in
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The output is compact JSON (no whitespace) with object keys sorted lexicographically (alphabetically). Every output line ends with a newline character.

trigger_keywords: how does overwrite work when key already exists
what happens when the key is already present and overwrite is false
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When overwrite=true: the key is written even if it already exists. When overwrite=false: existing keys are left unchanged, but missing keys are still added. patch_remove leaves locations that do not contain the key unchanged.

trigger_keywords: when is a pod considered finished
when does the interval have an end
what determines the end of a running interval
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The end is only reported once EVERY main container has finished. If any main container is still running or waiting, the result is state=Running (no end). Only when all main containers are finished does the state become Finished with start and end timestamps. Init containers do not block the end.

trigger_keywords: when is a selector expression invalid
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: An expression is malformed when: In/NotIn operators have no values list (or empty values), or when Exists/DoesNotExist operators have a values list present. The output is the exact line: error=malformed_label_selector

trigger_keywords: which metadata fields are removed during sanitize
what gets cleared in object sanitization
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Cleared scalar fields: creationTimestamp, deletionTimestamp, deletionGracePeriodSeconds, generation, managedFields, ownerReferences, resourceVersion, uid. Two annotations are dropped: kubectl.kubernetes.io/last-applied-configuration and deployment.kubernetes.io/revision. Empty fields (cleared scalars, empty annotations map) are omitted entirely from output.

trigger_keywords: what happens when both inputs are null for min_some
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: If both a and b are null (absent), result=none. If one is null, return the non-null one. If both present, return the smaller. The key distinction is null is ignored (not treated as the minimum).

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: handled the same way as our existing resource-lifecycle module
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The pod active-interval logic in golden/lib/k8s/container_state.rs — specifically the ActiveInterval enum (Empty, Running, Finished) and its comparison partial order.

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific information about that constraint. Please check the start.md for the full specification or look at the test cases in rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ for examples.

feature1_1_json_patch_add.json.description: Apply an additive change to a JSON document at a path that uses the '*' wildcard token to fan out across every element of an array. The path is a slash-separated JSON pointer in which a single '*' segment means 'every element of the array at this position'. For each location the path resolves to (which must be a JSON object), a given key/value pair is inserted. An 'overwrite' flag controls collisions: when true, the value is written even if the key already exists; when false, an existing key is left untouched while missing keys are still added. The whole (mutated) document is emitted as compact JSON with object keys sorted lexicographically.

feature1_2_json_patch_remove.json.description: Apply a removal change to a JSON document at a path that uses the '*' wildcard token to fan out across every element of an array. The path is a slash-separated JSON pointer in which a single '*' segment means 'every element of the array at this position'. For each location the path resolves to (which must be a JSON object), the entry under the given key is deleted if present; locations that do not contain the key are left unchanged. The whole (mutated) document is emitted as compact JSON with object keys sorted lexicographically.

feature2_label_selector_match.json.description: Evaluate whether a set of key/value labels satisfies a label selector. A selector may contain set-based 'matchExpressions' and/or an equality-based 'matchLabels' map; an object matches only if ALL requirements hold. Each expression has a key, an operator, and an optional list of values. The operators are: 'In' (key present and its value is one of the listed values), 'NotIn' (key absent, or present with a value not in the list), 'Exists' (key present), 'DoesNotExist' (key absent). 'In'/'NotIn' require a non-empty values list and 'Exists'/'DoesNotExist' require NO values list; violating that makes the expression malformed. The 'matchLabels' map requires each listed key to be present with exactly the listed value. Output is 'matched=true' or 'matched=false' for a valid evaluation, or the normalized error 'error=malformed_label_selector' when an expression breaks the values-presence rule.

feature3_pod_active_interval.json.description: Compute the active interval of a pod from its container statuses. The pod has an optional ordered list of init containers and a list of main containers; each container reports a state that is either running (with a start timestamp, in epoch seconds), finished (with start and end timestamps), or waiting (no timestamps). The pod's interval is derived as follows: the start is the EARLIEST start timestamp seen across all containers (init and main); the end is the LATEST finish timestamp, but only once EVERY main container has finished (if any main container is still running or waiting, the pod has no end yet). The result is one of three shapes: 'Empty' when no container has started, 'Running' with a start timestamp when started but not all main containers have finished, or 'Finished' with start and end timestamps when all main containers have finished.

feature4_interval_comparison.json.description: Compare two pod active-interval values under a partial order designed for safe monotonic updates. Each value is one of 'Empty', 'Running' with a start timestamp, or 'Finished' with start and end timestamps. The right operand may also be absent (null), representing 'no value yet'. Equality holds when both sides represent the same shape and timestamps (and 'Empty' is considered equal to an absent value). The ordering rules are: 'Empty' (or absent) is less than any started value; a 'Running' value is less than a 'Finished' value ONLY when they share the same start timestamp, otherwise the two are incomparable; two 'Running' values are equal iff equal, else incomparable; two 'Finished' values are equal iff both timestamps match, else incomparable. Any started value is greater than 'Empty'/absent. Output reports 'equal=<bool>' and 'ordering=<less|greater|equal|incomparable>', where 'incomparable' means the two values have no defined order.

feature5_min_optional.json.description: Compute the minimum of two optional integer values using 'absent-tolerant' semantics: unlike a naive comparison where an absent value would dominate, here an absent operand is simply ignored. If both operands are present, the smaller is returned; if exactly one is present, that one is returned; if both are absent, the result is absent. Output is 'result=<n>' for a present result or 'result=none' when both inputs are absent.

feature6_metadata_sanitize.json.description: Sanitize a cluster object's metadata so it can be re-applied as a fresh, declarative manifest, and stamp it with an explicit type. All server-populated or instance-specific metadata fields are cleared: creation timestamp, deletion timestamp, deletion grace period, generation, managed fields, owner references, resource version, and uid. From the annotations map, two tool-injected annotations are dropped (the last-applied-configuration annotation and the deployment-revision annotation) while all other annotations are preserved. Finally the object's type is set from the supplied api version and kind. Output is the resulting object emitted as compact JSON with object keys sorted lexicographically; fields that became empty are omitted entirely.
